## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two producers write to the same key at the same time — what's the order?"**
**The mechanism:** same key → same partition (the partitioner hashes the key), and a partition has a single leader that assigns a strictly increasing offset to each append. So the two writes are serialized by the leader in arrival order; whoever the leader appends first gets the lower offset.
**🗣️ In plain words:** same key always goes to the same drawer, and the drawer has one scribe who stamps a page number on arrival — so there's always a clear "first."
**💬 One-liner:** "Same key, same partition, single leader assigns offsets — order is whatever the leader saw first."

**❓ "What happens to acknowledged data if the leader crashes right after acking?"**
**The mechanism:** with `acks=all` and `min.insync=2`, the ack only went out *after* the ISR (≥2 machines) had the record. The controller elects a new leader *from the ISR*, which by definition has that record. Zero loss.
**🗣️ In plain words:** we only said "got it" once a backup already had a copy, so a backup takes over with the data intact.
**💬 One-liner:** "Acked data is always on the in-sync set, and we only promote from the in-sync set — so a leader crash loses nothing."

**❓ "Can a consumer process the same message twice?"**
**The mechanism:** yes — that's the at-least-once contract. A consumer commits its offset *after* processing; if it crashes in between, it reprocesses on restart. We make this safe with idempotent consumers that dedupe by a natural key in a TTL'd dedupe store.
**🗣️ In plain words:** a crash can make a reader re-read the last message, so readers are built to ignore anything they've already handled.
**💬 One-liner:** "At-least-once means duplicates are possible — we close the window with idempotent processing, not by promising delivery is perfect."

**❓ "A whole AZ goes down — what's the impact?"**
**The mechanism:** RF=3 spreads replicas across 3 AZs, so one AZ loss takes ~1/3 of leaders and replicas. The controller re-elects leaders in the surviving 2 AZs; writes pause for seconds, ISR stays ≥2 for most partitions, no acked data is lost.
**🗣️ In plain words:** lose one building and the other two carry on after a brief blip.
**💬 One-liner:** "Three copies across three buildings means one building can vanish with a few seconds of failover and zero data loss."

**❓ "What if a consumer falls way behind — does it lose data?"**
**The mechanism:** consumer lag (high-water mark minus committed offset) grows but is harmless until it exceeds retention; we alarm well before that, and tiered storage extends the deadline by keeping cold segments cheaply in object storage. A consumer stalled past retention loses its un-read data — accepted only for low-tier topics.
**🗣️ In plain words:** a slow reader has about a week of runway; we page on-call long before the oldest pages get thrown out.
**💬 One-liner:** "Lag is fine up to retention — ~a week of runway — and we alarm early; tiered storage buys more."

**❓ "How do you handle a flash flood of produce traffic — a retry storm?"**
**The mechanism:** producer quotas (per-client byte-rate token buckets) throttle over-budget clients via delayed responses, which back-pressures their bounded send buffers up to the app. Under cluster-wide overload we shed low-priority topics (debug/metrics) first, protecting money/CDC. Trigger = broker queue depth + produce p99.
**🗣️ In plain words:** we cap how fast each app can push, and if the whole cluster is drowning we throttle the least-important traffic first.
**💬 One-liner:** "Bounded buffers plus per-client quotas plus priority load-shedding — never an unbounded queue, and we protect the correctness path."

**❓ "Can the same logical event be published twice by a retrying producer?"**
**The mechanism:** the idempotent producer stamps each record with `(producerId, partition, sequence)`. The leader tracks the last sequence per producer-partition and rejects a duplicate sequence, so a retry after a lost ack is dedup'd server-side.
**🗣️ In plain words:** each send carries a counter, and the broker ignores a counter it's already seen, so a retry can't double-write.
**💬 One-liner:** "Idempotent producer sequence numbers mean a retried publish is silently dropped by the leader — no double-append."

**❓ "How do you stop a malicious or buggy client from reading another team's topic?"**
**The mechanism:** the connection is authenticated via mTLS/SASL (identity is never client-supplied), and per-topic ACLs check this *principal* against PRODUCE/CONSUME permission on *this specific topic* on every connection — object-level authz, not just authn.
**🗣️ In plain words:** we check both *who you are* and *whether you're allowed to touch this specific topic*, every time.
**💬 One-liner:** "mTLS identity plus per-topic ACLs — authn answers 'who', authz answers 'allowed on THIS topic'."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut it?"**
**The mechanism:** cross-AZ network (~$2.5M/mo) dominates — replication and consume traffic crossing AZ boundaries, billed per GB. The fix is rack/AZ-aware placement plus follower-fetch and consumer-fetch from the *closest* (same-AZ) replica, plus tiering cold data to object storage (~4× cheaper than NVMe).
**🗣️ In plain words:** the biggest bill is moving bytes between buildings, so we keep reads and replication within a building wherever we can.
**💬 One-liner:** "Cross-AZ traffic is the CDN-egress of messaging — same-AZ fetch and tiered storage are the big levers."

**❓ "Can you oversell partition ordering — i.e. can records for one key end up out of order?"**
**The mechanism:** only if you (a) increase partition count (changes key→partition mapping for new writes) or (b) run with `max.in.flight > 1` *without* the idempotent producer (a retry can reorder). With the idempotent producer, in-flight requests are sequenced, so order holds even with retries.
**🗣️ In plain words:** order is safe as long as you don't reshuffle partitions mid-stream and you turn on the idempotent producer.
**💬 One-liner:** "Per-key order holds unless you repartition or disable idempotency — both are explicit, avoidable choices."

**❓ "How would you add true exactly-once across consume-process-produce later?"**
**The mechanism:** add a **transaction coordinator** — the consumer reads, processes, produces output *and* commits its input offset inside a single broker transaction; consumers downstream read with `read_committed` so they only see committed output. It reuses the existing log + a transaction-state topic.
**🗣️ In plain words:** wrap "read, transform, write, bookmark" in one all-or-nothing transaction so a crash can't leave it half-done.
**💬 One-liner:** "A transaction coordinator makes consume-process-produce atomic — opt-in per pipeline because it costs throughput."

**❓ "How would you support multi-region / disaster recovery?"**
**The mechanism:** async mirroring (a MirrorMaker-style consumer reads the source cluster and produces into a remote cluster) gives RPO = mirror lag (seconds) with no write-latency tax; or synchronous cross-region `acks=all` for RPO≈0 at the cost of cross-region latency on every write.
**🗣️ In plain words:** either copy data to another region a few seconds behind, or write to both regions before acking — speed vs zero-loss.
**💬 One-liner:** "Async mirror for cheap DR with seconds of RPO, or synchronous cross-region writes for zero loss and a latency tax — pick per the requirement."

**❓ "Why a log instead of a traditional message queue?"**
**The mechanism:** a log decouples retention from consumption — multiple independent consumer groups read the same data at their own pace, and any can replay by resetting its offset. A queue deletes on ack, so there's one consumer's worth of data and no replay.
**🗣️ In plain words:** the log keeps a week of history that many readers can re-read independently; a queue throws each message away once one reader handles it.
**💬 One-liner:** "A log gives replay and many independent readers; a queue gives point-to-point delivery and forgets — different jobs."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is a durable, replayable log: producers append events to partitioned topics, consumers read them by offset and can rewind. The design splits into two halves. The read half is consumer groups — a coordinator hands each consumer a slice of partitions, they pull records at their own pace and bookmark their position in an internal compacted offsets topic, and cooperative rebalancing reassigns only what must move when membership changes. The write half is partitioned, replicated append-only logs — each partition has a single leader for ordering and an in-sync replica set for durability; the hardest problem is never losing an acknowledged write, and the mechanism that solves it is the ISR with a minimum in-sync count, so we only acknowledge data that's safely on two machines and only ever fail over to a replica that already has it. The two halves connect through the partition log itself — the same append-only stream that producers write, followers replicate, and consumers replay. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: never lose an acknowledged write and never violate per-partition order, and trade everything else — latency, availability, even throughput — to protect those two."
